ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for trio-egg spinach stir-fry. Closest defensible anchor is cooked spinach food product (FOODON_03301710); egg components are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Spinach, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. The three-egg components (century egg, salted egg, fresh egg) are not individually represented in this code; the code covers the dominant vegetable base.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for trio-egg spinach stir-fry as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of trio eggs spinach at a Singapore hawker centre or zi char stall is approximately 150 g, representing a shared portion of stir-fried spinach topped with sliced century egg, salted egg, and fresh egg.

nutrient_source.title: Trio Eggs Spinach — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Trio Eggs Spinach (cr_id F14080058). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central egg and spinach component data where HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — spinach and non-starchy vegetable analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for trio eggs spinach as a composite dish. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to cooked non-starchy leafy greens (spinach, GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The egg components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially affect the GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 3.25 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 0.7, confirming very low glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted egg, century egg, and soy sauce (368 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced soy sauce and fewer preserved egg components, as the salted egg and century egg are the primary sodium drivers in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.25 g/100 g) and low GI (~15) with moderate protein (3.43 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (1.17 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Trio eggs spinach is suitable for blood glucose management due to its very low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index; however, the stir-frying oil adds caloric density, so portion control remains important for weight management in type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (368 mg/100 g) and moderate fat (6.35 g/100 g) with estimated saturated fat ~1.8 g/100 g; spinach provides cardioprotective potassium, iron, and antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The spinach base is cardioprotective, but the high sodium from preserved eggs and soy sauce is a concern for cardiovascular disease; patients should request reduced seasoning and limit preserved egg portions to manage sodium and dietary cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Spinach provides anti-inflammatory micronutrients (vitamin C, vitamin K, iron, calcium); saturated fat well below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spinach is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory nutrient profile, but patients should request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium, which can worsen tissue swelling, and note that spinach is high in oxalates relevant to certain RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Trio Eggs Spinach (三蛋菠菜, San Dan Bo Cai) is a popular Singaporean Chinese zi char and hawker dish consisting of stir-fried spinach topped or cooked with three distinct types of egg: century egg (pidan, alkaline-cured preserved duck egg with a dark, gelatinous texture and pungent umami flavour), salted duck egg (brined, with a firm, salty yolk), and fresh chicken egg (lightly scrambled or poached into the dish). The spinach is typically wok-fried with garlic and a small amount of oil, then seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The result is a dish with contrasting textures — silky wilted spinach, creamy salted egg yolk, firm century egg, and soft fresh egg — and a rich, savoury, slightly salty flavour profile. Nutritionally, the dish provides 84 kcal per 100 g, with moderate fat (6.35 g), low carbohydrate (3.25 g), and useful protein (3.43 g). Dietary fibre (1.17 g/100 g) and micronutrients including calcium (68 mg), iron (1.02 mg), and potassium (~280 mg) are contributed primarily by the spinach. Sodium is elevated at 368 mg/100 g due to the preserved egg components and soy-based seasoning, making this dish a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The glycaemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan or halal due to the egg types and potential oyster sauce use. It is commonly served as a shared side dish at Singapore hawker centres, zi char stalls, and Chinese restaurants.

Sodium content of trio eggs spinach

meal_context: shared side dish at zi char stall

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080058) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data. GI estimated by analogy to cooked spinach from the University of Sydney GI Database. Multilingual names verified against standard Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi translations for this well-known Singaporean dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish (trio eggs spinach) is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a combined dish entry as of 2026. cholesterol_mg set to -1: HPB source does not report cholesterol for this dish; cholesterol is expected to be present from egg components but no approved-source value is available for the composite. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this dish and no approved composite-dish source provides these values. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: trans fat is absent in this dish as it is prepared with standard vegetable cooking oil and no hydrogenated fats are used. potassium_mg value of 280 mg/100 g is an estimate based on spinach potassium content from USDA FoodData Central (spinach, cooked, USDA SR Legacy #11458) and is documented as approximate. saturated_fat_g of 1.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked eggs and spinach; HPB does not report saturated fat for this dish. Input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian', 'plant_based', 'low_calorie', and 'keto'; 'plant_based' has been removed as the dish contains animal-derived eggs; 'keto' is retained as the dish has very low net carbohydrates (3.25 g/100 g). is_halal set to false as century egg and salted egg preparation methods and the potential use of oyster sauce may not conform to halal certification standards without explicit certification.
